Home Studio\n: ReZoned: Adaptive Housing on Underused Church Land — British Columbia Institute of Technology
Tags: Housing design, Housing Production, Shared Spaces, Programmed Circulation, Flexible Spaces, Infill Housing
Project Description:
Hamlet is the little village within a village, located on an underused church lot in Vancouver. The project revitalizes the site by organizing non-market housing around a pedestrian street defined by the architecture and landscape design.
Inspired by the character of a medieval village, the housing is expressed as charming micro-towers on top of a podium. The towers frame the site edges, while the podium establishes a height buffer, bringing the project to a human-scale. A cut in the building forms a plaza marking a primary site entrance. This opening responds to and connects with the neighbouring ISS building, while also welcoming people into the site, framing a view of the church, and filtering movement from street to pedestrian space.
The plaza functions like an outdoor room, enclosed by shared indoor amenities including a communal kitchen, dining, and living space. Above, the semi-public podium provides a quiet layer of shared space with planting, trees, and informal seating supporting everyday social use.
Through the layering of public, semi-public, and private space, the project creates a dense but connected environment that brings a village-like sense of community into this contemporary, urban context.

Home Studio\n: ReZoned: Adaptive Housing on Underused Church Land — British Columbia Institute of Technology
Tags: Housing design, Prefabrication, Housing Production, Shared Spaces, Co-Housing, Courtyard Housing, Towers
Project Description:
ARC + AXIS is a mixed-income housing proposal on the former First Christian Reformed Church site in Vancouver, near Commercial–Broadway Station. Located beside the Grandview Cut and adjacent to ISSofBC, the project responds to a rapidly changing neighbourhood shaped by transit access, migration, redevelopment pressure, and housing affordability. It views housing not only as shelter, but as civic infrastructure that can support dignity, belonging, and community life.

The project evolved from an earlier concept, guided by the values of welcome, preservation, storytelling, and connection. The most important of these became Preserve and Embrace. Rather than clearing the site, the proposal begins with the existing mature trees as the foundation of the design. Trees are retained and become generators of building form, open space, and circulation. The architecture bends and opens around them, allowing nature to remain central to daily life.

The name reflects this idea. The arc represents curved forms that wrap around trees, shape courtyards, and create welcoming edges. The axis provides clear structure, circulation, and efficient organization. Together, these systems balance environmental sensitivity with practical urban housing.

Two coordinated residential buildings frame a hierarchy of public, semi-public, and private open spaces. Shared courtyards centered around preserved trees become gathering spaces, while pathways weave through landscaped areas to create connection and discovery.
The project includes a mix of one-bedroom, two-bedroom, and three-bedroom homes, with refugee housing units integrated into the broader community. Ground-floor amenity spaces activate the site, while bicycle storage and servicing are carefully embedded into the building base.

ARC + AXIS proposes that density and preservation can coexist. By building with the landscape instead of against it, the project transforms existing trees into the heart of a resilient and humane urban community.

Project Description: My project is part of a larger masterplan that includes a classmate who developed their mixed use housing project directly beside mine. Some of our design principles included: connected courtyards/plazas with exposure ranging from public to semi-private. Following this logic included an extensive green roof winding upwards from the most public plaza, incorporating community gardens. My site is oriented around the most semi-private courtyard, with places to read and connect in small groups. Sloped roofs direct rainwater towards the pebble gardens, located in the center of this courtyard. The massing for this project is comprised of smaller, broken up modules. These modules of 2-3 units per floor are connected with horizontal circulation facing south, and facing the courtyard. The walkways are designed with a jogging pattern, creating wider places to pause or socialize, without being in the way of others circulating. With carbon sequestration in mind, post and beam framing provides the structural frame, with CLT panels spanning beams to create floors, roofs, and even the walkways. Due to moisture realities, the top of the CLT walkways are finished with a waterproof membrane and an exposed aggregate concrete topper. The protruding wooden structure, cedar shake siding, and vertical shading screens create a perfect place for nature and humanity to make themselves at home.

Image 2 Alt/Description: Design principles, master planning, and massing. These masterplanning was shared work done with my classmate who designed the buildings beside me. Her buildings are the ones on the left (East) of the site, my buildings are on the right (West). The buildings seen in the blue circulation diagram are the buildings that I developed after our combined master planning exercise.

Project Description:
This studio project is the redevelopment of the site currently occupied by the First
Christian Reformed Church of Vancouver, its parking lot, and the former rectory. The site is situated within a rapidly transforming transit-oriented area. It lies near a major SkyTrain station and rapid bus corridor, adjacent to the Grandview Cut, and immediately beside ISS of BC, a globally recognized facility serving recent immigrants and refugees. The surrounding blocks are subject to significant redevelopment pressure, including recently approved and proposed high-rise projects.

In teams of 2, a mini master plan was created to establish public, semi public, and private zones of the site. In addition, replace the existing church with a new, free-standing church building located on one of the three parcels, dedicate the remaining two parcels primarily to housing, with one community amenity space, and ensure that each parcel can function as an independently phased development. Each parcel was then designed individually.

Next Steps, situated in proposed lot 2, is centered around micro mobility, community, and intergenerational living. Situated along a major bike path, It is a hub for bike commuters looking to maintain, fix, and get into cycling. This project has removed private exterior balconies and uses communal rooftop terraces to encourage chance encounters with residents. Next Steps also incorporates a diverse range of unit types to accommodate intergenerational living. In addition, the building incorporates dedicated refugee housing placed near the roof top decks improving visibility to long-term residents and make social interactions less of a chore.

At the end of it all, what Next Steps really wants to inspire ownership, pride, and shared identity for all residents and community members.

Tags: Co-Housing, Co-Living
Project Description:
The function behind this project is to provide housing to families, as well as provide a limited number of units to aid the ISSofBC located in the next site over to house refugees. The tenants are distinctly groups that needs safety, security, and protection, as well as simultaneously interactions with the broader community for proper growth. Which is why for this project, it is very important to distinguish the public spaces, the private spaces, and the interaction spaces.

The basic massing is a cluster of vertical buildings centering around a raised podium level. The podium level would be a distinct private space where the residents will interact only with each other and guests they are comfortable with. Underneath the podium houses services, storage, and bicycle parking. At the podium level is a walkway which connects my building to my immediate neighbour. Also on the level of the podium are the residents' amenity spaces: gym, quiet lounge/study, and living room/hangout space.

The idea is to provide a very spacious communal living room instead of having the floor space divided amongst the individual suites. Because individually, the living rooms are less spacious thus less useful, and certain families will probably not make as full use out of it anyways. Though each unit will still have their own kitchen and smaller living and dining rooms.

At the ground level is an open playground that services the children living in the building as well as children from the neighbourhood. This is a distinct public area where the residents interact with not just themselves, but with people in the broader community. The playground is accompanied with a indoor living room that connects directly to the podium level living room so that neighbours can be invited in when they become friends.

Project Description:
The project is a redevelopment of a site in Vancouver currently occupied by the First Christian Reformed Church of Vancouver and its parking lot with residential housing. My design focuses on building a community within the site without exluding the surrounding neighborhood and the church. As exterior spaces are important to make residents connect, the project includes a courtyard, exterior circulation and a roof garden that is connecting every building. The roof gardens are an extension of the exterior. 
While community spaces are important, privacy should not be forgotten. That is why each building is rotated in a way to not view into each others apartment. The chruch is located at the main street to be easily accessed for everyone. Right next to is a community kitchen which can be used for public events, the chruch and the residents.

Project Description: After analysing the area I was planning to build my residences, I decided to focus mainly on the creation of public spaces deeply connected to the private ones. I wanted people who live in the building to feel sorrounded by the community and to do this, I created exterior corridors for the circulation and a mix of units inside the buildings. Moreover I planned to put all the communal spaces on the ground floor, to make them accessible to everyone. This is how I imagine the future of this area: a place where the community is stronger than the individuality and makes everyone feel part of it.

Project Description:
Located at the corner of 11th Street and Victoria Drive, 'Elevated Blocks' is a six-storey social co-housing project that focuses on user experience through openness and shared living. The building features a lifted wooden structure, freeing the ground plane and enhancing pedestrian circulation.

The project is rooted in its three guiding principles: transparency, connection, and community. Spaces are designed to feel visually and physically open, allowing relationships to form not just between residents but also with the surrounding neighborhood. The building contains a shared kitchen and dining space for the neighborhood, acting as a daily gathering point that extends beyond the building itself.

The site itself contains two residential buildings, an existing church, a community garden, and a playground. Rather than treating these as separate elements, the project connects them into a continuous social landscape—one that encourages overlap, interaction, and a stronger sense of community.
Image 1 Alt/Description: Ground Floor Plan.
Image 1 Caption: Due to its location, the building is shaped by its surrounding environment. Capitalizing off of its proximity to the Immigrant Services Society of BC (ISS) and the church to create a shared plaza between the three buildings.
Image 2 Alt/Description: Floor Plans 2-6.
Image 2 Caption: The building uses single point access for each 'square' ensuring that corner unit that has access to sunlight.
Image 3 Alt/Description: South Elevation (From 11th Street).
Image 3 Caption: From 11th Street, Elevated Blocks reveals its relationship to the broader site. Designed to minimize disruption to the surrounding environment, the building does so through its modest scale and integration of green space.
Image 4 Alt/Description: East Elevation (From Victoria Drive).
Image 4 Caption: From Victoria Drive, Elevated Blocks presents a simple, grounded form with a warm timber façade and a consistent window rhythm. The lifted structure creates a recessed ground level, opening space for movement and reinforcing the building’s connection to the public realm. Additionally, we're able to see its relationship with the shared plaza.
Image 5 Alt/Description: Section Cut A-A'
Image 5 Caption: A strong sense of symmetry organizes the building, aligning the structural grid with vertical circulation on either side.
Image 6 Alt/Description: Section Cut B-B'
Image 7 Alt/Description: Typical Unit Layouts (3 Bed, 2 Beds, 1 Bed).
Image 7 Caption: Each unit is compliant with BC Housing design standards.

Tags: Housing design, Shared Spaces, Programmed Circulation, Flexible Spaces, Co-Housing, Co-Living, Courtyard Housing, Agriculture and Gardening
Project Description: The Cascade Garden is a proposal for social housing focusing on connectivity through a range of communal areas and ecologically blossoming outdoor spaces. Amenities including a community kitchen and a social study space are located on the ground floor for access to all, while a courtyard is offered south of the building with a playground for families with children and a gazebo for residents to commune. The stepped-up form brings each end of the building down to the human scale, and individual roofs offer moments of pause via private gardens. Surrounding vines add to a sense of security as well as a great view of the mountains to the north – coupled with planters for each unit – drawing people to these open spaces to foster greater community engagement. Through these aspects, this proposal hopes to grant residents with a safe and comforting space they can call home, and the community as a whole with spaces of interaction amongst neighbours.
Image 1 Alt/Description: Site plan showing the two proposed buildings on the site, one on the North side and one on the South. The building I'm proposing is situated on the North, with the ground floor consisting of a communal kitchen, services, a casual study space, a few units, and bike storage. A courtyard through the centre of the site offers a playground and an informal gazebo for communal engagement.
Image 2 Alt/Description: The concept of the building consists of multiple blocks connected together in sequence. Each block creates stacking units with a roof garden topping off each one. These gardens are oriented in a way that mimics the site itself, with planters in the form of blocks, and a path that draws a visual connection between each of the roofs.
Image 3 Alt/Description: Units in the building range from 1 bedroom to 3 bedrooms. All units were made with the entry leading straight into the living room, the most communal space, moving to the dining room, then the kitchen. While bathrooms are centrally located in each unit, bedrooms are always kept to the outside. Furthermore, each unit is given a south-facing balcony that overlooks the courtyard below.
Image 4 Alt/Description: The Grandview Cut, a large consideration in this project, seen on the left side of the image, is an excavation done to the north-east of the site to allow a train to run by. To respond to this the building is stepped-up to a higher point at that end in order to act as a sound buffer that keeps the east side of the site quiet. On the west side, the stepping-down allows people along the street to see the activity taking place on the rooftop gardens above and invites them to explore further into the site.
Image 5 Alt/Description: The elevation gives a better view of the materiality of the building. The main materials used were horizontal wood siding as well as black aluminum panels. I wanted the exterior stairs to stand out, hence why they have a contrasting white aluminum siding. The greenery in the form of vines on the roof acts as the wall to roof transition and rounds out the building, having landscaping at the ground level as well as the top.
Image 6 Alt/Description: Stairs are a main part of the facade, but they are also situated in a prime spot for communal interaction. As exterior stairs, they are open to the courtyard, and the contrast in materiality draws peoples eyes to the people using them, leading to further interaction. In terms of circulation through the buildings, every two blocks are shared by one point access stair and elevator. All access points are on the courtyard side of building so that everyone entering and exiting their units will meet within the communal space.
Image 7 Alt/Description: This project consisted of two partners creating two designs on this shared site. Thus, an original master plan had to be made before the design could be realised. Therefore, I constructed a 1:200 scale massing model to show the original idea we had going into the design, prior to changes that were made as the design evolved.
Image 8 Alt/Description: The final model shown is a 1:24 scale of a portion of the facade of the building. It shows the wood and aluminum cladding, as well as the balconies stacked vertically serving each unit. The right side of my image shows a cut through the stairs, with vertical wood slats that extent to the height of the building. What this model emphasizes, and can be seen to a degree in the shadows, is that the slats are angled with the long side towards the balconies so that residents using the stairs are blocked from seeing directly onto the adjacent balconies, but are still given a sense that the stairs are airy and open to the surrounding courtyard.

Image Description/Alt Text\n: Axonometric View of The Interstice
Home Studio\n: ReZoned: Adaptive Housing on Underused Church Land — British Columbia Institute of Technology
Tags: Housing theory, Co-Housing, Courtyard Housing
Project Description:
The Interstice reimagines a site currently occupied by a church, proposing a redevelopment that better serves both the congregation and the surrounding community. The project introduces shared spaces designed to support community use, in collaboration with the nearby Immigrant Services Society of BC (ISS of BC).

As one of three residential buildings within a broader master plan, two of which are intended for future phases, The Interstice focuses on providing housing for those seeking refuge. The unit mix prioritizes two- and three-bedroom layouts to accommodate families and larger households.

The building employs a single-loaded corridor strategy, enabling cross ventilation for passive cooling within the units. This exterior corridor also creates a generous overhang, offering shade during the summer while allowing solar access in the winter. Beyond its environmental performance, the corridor functions as a shared social space, encouraging interaction and fostering a sense of community among residents.

Outdoor areas are shaped by the building footprints, responding directly to site conditions and user needs. A tiered master plan minimizes shadowing between structures while maintaining adequate space for housing and essential services.

The Interstice demonstrates how thoughtful redevelopment can give back to the community, creating opportunities for connection, support, and shared public life through the spaces it defines.
Image 1 Alt/Description: The overall master plan was formed from a response to the location of the ISS of BC, providing easy access into the site from the north west corner. The building heights tier down to ease the proposal to the existing 3-4 storey buildings, while also minimizing the shadowing that the buildings produce on the site. Existing and proposed trees are perserved through the cuts made in the building form.
Image 2 Alt/Description: The first floor provides spaces for community engagement, such as a flex space, community kitchen, and gym. The first floor also provides space for a loading bay, mailroom, and bike storage.
Image 3 Alt/Description: Levels 2-7 host the majority of the residential units. The mix includes studio, 2-bedroom, and 3-bedroom units. Access to these units are provided via an exterior corridor on the south side of the building.
Image 4 Alt/Description: This section provides context to the spatial environment of the outdoor space created by the massing of The Interstice in relation to the human scale. It also provides how the landscaping shapes the space on the ground level.
Image 5 Alt/Description: This detail demonstrates the constructability of the proposal. The project shows a hybrid mass timber approach, utilizing CLT for the floor assemblies and light steel framing for the walls. This detail also demostrates the attachment of the exterior corridor and guardrail to the facade.

Image Description/Alt Text\n: Street view of the site facing the church and the entrance to one of the project buildings.
Home Studio\n: ReZoned: Adaptive Housing on Underused Church Land — British Columbia Institute of Technology
Tags: Housing design, Co-Housing, Infill Housing
Project Description:
The project began with the redesign of an underused site containing a church, a single-family house, and a parking lot located at the intersection of Victoria Drive and East 11th Avenue.

Working in teams of two, we developed a mini masterplan that divides the site into three parcels: the church and two new buildings. The proposal considers phased construction, with each parcel developed at different times, while exploring how built form can establish a hierarchy of spaces in response to the site’s social and physical context.

The project then transitioned into an individual phase, where each building design reflects and reinforces the core ideas established in the masterplan.
Image 1 Alt/Description: Physical model of the site with student's project on it
Image 1 Caption: The masterplan responds to the site’s shape and surrounding context. The west building, Elevated Blocks (by Angelica Chua), maintains a horizontal orientation, while the east building, Discovery, continues the angular geometry of the adjacent Immigrant Services Society of BC building (ISS) and the Millennium SkyTrain tracks. Positioned on the southern portion of the site, the west building creates open space between itself and the ISS building. This area serves as a shared open space for the ISS and establishes a grand entry sequence to the centrally located church. The east building acts as a sound barrier, buffering the site and surrounding neighborhood from train noise. Together, the three buildings define a semi-public open space along the southern edge of the site, designated for gardening and a playground. This quieter shared space is embraced by all three buildings, creating a buffer from the more public areas of the site.
Image 2 Alt/Description: Site Plan
Image 2 Caption: Inspired by the walkways and paths of Olympic Village, the plaza features a fountain and landscaped routes leading toward the church. On the southern portion of the site, a more semi-public area is designated for residents, including gardens and a playground.
Image 3 Alt/Description: Ground Floor Plan
Image 3 Caption: Discovery’s ground floor is designed to be resident-focused, with amenities that encourage connection and community. It provides spaces for neighbors to get to know one another, host small events, and gather with people from adjacent communities, including the ISS, the church, and nearby buildings. A guest unit is also included for residents to accommodate visiting friends and family.
Image 4 Alt/Description: Typical floor plan from level 2 to 6
Image 4 Caption: Discovery consists of two single-exit towers with a mix of unit types on each floor, including one-bedroom, two-bedroom, and three-bedroom units. This variety encourages diverse interactions among residents on every level. The building includes five refugee units, which are not fixed to specific locations. Instead, residents can choose any available three-bedroom unit across different floors, allowing for greater flexibility and integration within the community. All units on each floor share a single hallway, promoting daily encounters and a sense of immediate neighborhood. This layout encourages residents to become familiar with one another, fostering comfort, connection, and opportunities for casual interaction.
Image 5 Alt/Description: Typical unit plans of 1 bed, 2 bed, and 3 bed units
Image 5 Caption: Each unit is designed with a clear separation between private bedrooms and shared living spaces, along with efficient layouts for kitchens and bathrooms. In some units, the entry is located near the bedrooms; however, priority is given to the living areas, which are oriented to the south to maximize natural light and provide access to balconies.
Image 6 Alt/Description: Rooftop plan
Image 6 Caption: Units do not include in-suite laundry. Instead, a communal laundry area is located on the rooftop, inspired by Nightingale 1 in Australia. The rooftop also features a shared garden and viewing area, allowing residents to relax or spend time under a canopy while waiting for their laundry. This setup encourages encounters beyond each floor, helping residents connect and become more aware of the broader community within the building.
Image 8 Caption: Discovery’s balconies alternate on each floor, creating opportunities for visual connection and interaction between residents. The building façade features fibre cement panels with a dark wood–like texture, giving the building a warm, approachable character.
Image 9 Caption: Rooftop view of Discovery, illustrating a shared space that fosters social interaction while framing views of the surrounding context.
Image 10 Alt/Description: Facade and balcony detail of the building
Image 10 Caption: This façade detail model illustrates the balcony design used in Discovery. Perforated balcony panels are used to support the building’s core concept of connection. With balconies alternating between floors, the perforation provides a balance of privacy and openness, offering more screening than glass guardrails while still allowing light and views for residents using the space.

Image Description/Alt Text\n: Mountain Facade
Home Studio\n: ReZoned: Adaptive Housing on Underused Church Land — British Columbia Institute of Technology
Tags: Cooperative housing, Housing design, Option 9, Shared Spaces, Programmed Circulation, Flexible Spaces, Co-Housing, Courtyard Housing, Bar Buildings, Mixed Use
Project Description:
Rise Again is a seven-story residential building that explores a missing middle housing approach, with a focus on community, rest, and connection. The number seven is intentional. It draws inspiration from the idea of rest, particularly the concept in Christianity where the seventh day is a day of rest. This idea is translated into architecture as a building that supports both daily life and moments of pause.

At the ground level, the first floor is designed as a shared community space.
It includes a community hall and kitchen, which act as the social heart of the building and allow residents to gather, share meals, and build relationships.

Bike parking is also located on this level, promoting sustainable transportation and supporting everyday convenience.

The upper six floors are dedicated to residential units. Each floor contains eight units, creating a consistent and efficient layout.

The repeated mountain motif across the building represents the mountains that the building itself partially blocks.

The façade becomes a symbolic gesture, reflecting the presence of the mountains and maintaining a visual connection to the landscape.

This idea is reinforced at the rooftop level, where residents have access to shared outdoor space with direct views of the mountains. This creates a full experience, from symbolic representation on the façade to real physical connection at the top.

The building is situated between two existing structures, which can create a sense of density and enclosure. To respond to this condition, I chose a palette of white and yellow.

White helps reflect light and makes the building feel more open and less heavy within the tight urban context. 

Yellow introduces warmth and brightness, helping to bring light into the space and create a more uplifting and welcoming atmosphere.

Together, these colours soften the density of the surroundings and enhance the overall experience of the building.

Image 2 Alt/Description: Mountain Facade
Image 3 Alt/Description: White and Yellow

Image 5 Alt/Description: Building on Site (Side)

Image Description/Alt Text\n: View of South Face
Home Studio\n: ReZoned: Adaptive Housing on Underused Church Land — British Columbia Institute of Technology
Tags: Decolonization, Housing design, Towers, Infill Housing
Project Description: This project is a reimagined version of the traditional medieval village. Tilted towers practices framed architecture that features a 2-storey podium level with recessed maximum 8-storey towers, creating a sense of human scale. This central lane creates framing views that ties the ISS (Immigration Service Society) to the church neighbouring residential communities on the eastern side. This project practices single point access carrying a variety of unit combinations to facilitate interaction among neighbours; three bedrooms adjacent to one bedrooms, two bedrooms adjacent to two bedrooms, and single room levels.
Image 1 Alt/Description: South Elevation
Image 1 Caption: This south elevation shows the connection of lower towers facing towards East 11th Ave with a white brick exterior and yellow fibre cement on the interior side. This same yellow is detailed in the window frames and underside of the balcony, providing a sense of hope and positivity for residents. In addition, the balconies are skillfully pushed inward to provide a seamless finish.
Image 2 Alt/Description: North Elevation
Image 2 Caption: This north elevation demonstrates higher towers located towards the rear of the site for acoustic buffering from the nearby trainline and for increase in unit density.
Image 3 Alt/Description: Section A-A
Image 3 Caption: This section demonstrates how residents flow through the building and experience day to day operations.
Image 4 Alt/Description: Section B-B
Image 4 Caption: This cross section displays inbuilt planter boxes in the recessed balconies that provide a space for individuality in each unit.
Image 5 Alt/Description: Core Unit Plan 1
Image 5 Caption: First floor plan of a 2 bedroom unit mixed with another 2 bedroom unit.
Image 6 Alt/Description: Core Unit Plan 2
Image 6 Caption: Fourth floor plan of a 3 bedroom unit mixed with a 1 bedroom unit in the towers. This facilitates interaction among residents with different family sizes.
Image 7 Caption: This site shows the connection of the ISS (north side), the church, and Co-op housing (eastern side).
Image 8 Caption: yThe lower level contains the amenity spaces towards the eastern side to facilitate connection towards the more residential portions of the neighbourhood. The multipurpose room is designed to be a quiet room, where closed off rooms are designated for residents to book. These rooms are for studying or praying. The rest of the space is open to the community as additional working space, especially for single parents working at home.
Image 9 Caption: Tilted Towers uses single access points to create cross ventilation throughout each of the towers and podium levels.
Image 10 Caption: Level 3 features a semi-public garden for residents to gather as an extension of their own units. These are accessible through each of the towers.

Image Description/Alt Text\n: Axonometric Drawing of Unilever Precinct Site
Home Studio\n: Counter Proposal Studio — Toronto Metropolitan University
Tags: Housing design, Shared Spaces, Flexible Spaces, Courtyard Housing
Project Description: Our project counters Cadillac Fariview's proposal of the Unilever Precinct site. Instead of massive office towers adjacent to single family housing neighbourhoods, we instead propose moderate density which is contextually sensitive. We reject CF's proposal for white- collar office jobs and instead harken back to the site's industrial past by promoting blue-collar work with new trade school. Utilizing the existing Unilever Soap factory, we preserve the industrial character of the site by adaptively reusing the building. The old industrial quiality is celebrated in its raw state with minimal yet intentional interventions. The trade school acts as an anchor for the new East Harbour Transit Hub attracting workers and visitors from all over Toronto and Beyond.

Image Description/Alt Text\n: View into the central mews illustrating the inward-facing organization of the project where residential entries and shared spaces activate public realm and support everyday community interaction. The image incorporates a series of concept diagrams outlining the core design moves: retaining the existing tree as a social anchor, establishing connections through the site, lifting the building mass, and grounding the project within its urban context
Home Studio\n: ReZoned: Adaptive Housing on Underused Church Land — British Columbia Institute of Technology
Tags: Housing design, Shared Spaces, Programmed Circulation, Flexible Spaces, Co-Housing, Row Housing, Infill Housing, Agriculture and Gardening
Project Description:
This village explores housing as a framework for community, centered on an inward-facing mews that fosters everyday social interaction. Rather than treating circulation as a purely functional element, the design repositions access as a social spine, where front doors, elevated walkways, and shared outdoor spaces collectively encourage informal encounters and cultivate belonging.

The site strategy is anchored by the retention of an existing mature tree, preserving a natural gathering point and organizes the public realm. From this anchor, the proposal establishes a deliberate gradient of public, semi-public, and private spaces, that supports both collective activity and individual retreat. The central mews serves as the primary organizing element, framed by residential units whose primary entrances face inward to enhance visibility, safety, and community engagement.

The building massing responds sensitively to context and program. A curved residential form defines the site's interior while lifting at strategic moments to introduce natural light, permeability, and vertical connections. Exterior walkways and terraces extend circulation into shared space, enabling residents to engage with the mews beyond their unit thresholds.

A diverse mix of unit types (studios, one, two, three-bedroom units) is distributed throughout the building to accommodate varied household needs. Integrated refugee housing is dispersed across multiple levels and locations to promote inclusion within the broader village rather than isolation. Proximity to shared amenities reinforces accessibility and social integration.

Precedents such as Peter Barber Architects' Edgewood Mews and Barcelona's Born Market inform the spatial and landscape strategies, particularly the use of simple forms, topography, and pedestrian-focused sequences to support flexible social use. Ultimately, Village Mews is a resilient system for fostering connection, community resilience, and shared urban life.
Image 1 Alt/Description: Image depicts diagram illustrating the relationship between existing and proposed buildings, identifying phases of development.
Image 2 Alt/Description: Image depicts distribution of refugee housing units across the site, intentionally dispersed across multiple buildings and levels to promote integration, equitable access to amenities, and avoidance of spatial isolation within the larger residential community.
Image 2 Caption: Refugee Housing Distribution and Integration Strategy

Image Description/Alt Text\n: AXONOMETRIC OF THE INTERSTICE
Home Studio\n: ReZoned: Adaptive Housing on Underused Church Land — British Columbia Institute of Technology
Tags: Housing design, Prefabrication, Shared Spaces, Co-Housing, Courtyard Housing, Infill Housing
Project Description:
The Interstice reimagines a site currently occupied by a church, proposing a redevelopment that better serves both the congregation and the surrounding community. The project introduces shared spaces designed to support community use, in collaboration with the nearby Immigrant Services Society of BC (ISS of BC).

As one of three residential buildings within a broader master plan, two of which are developed by Kyon Gicole, The Interstice focuses on providing housing for those seeking refuge. The unit mix prioritizes two- and three-bedroom layouts to accommodate families and larger households.

The building employs a single-loaded corridor strategy, enabling cross ventilation for passive cooling within the units. This exterior corridor also creates a generous overhang, offering shade during the summer while allowing solar access in the winter. Beyond its environmental performance, the corridor functions as a shared social space, encouraging interaction and fostering a sense of community among residents.

Outdoor areas are shaped by the building footprints, responding directly to site conditions and user needs. A tiered master plan minimizes shadowing between structures while maintaining adequate space for housing and essential services.

The Interstice demonstrates how thoughtful redevelopment can give back to the community, creating opportunities for connection, support, and shared public life through the spaces it defines.
Image 1 Alt/Description: Building masses were formed to invite people of the ISS of BC into the site. Tiered building heights minimize shading and provide a gradual transition from the proposed site to the existing 3-4 storey buildings. Lastly, voids were cut in the forms of the building as a response to the existing and proposed trees on the site.
Image 2 Alt/Description: The First Floor Plan features shared community spaces such as a gym, community kitchen, and flex space. Utilities such as a garbage room, mailroom, and bike parking are also located on this level.
Image 3 Alt/Description: A typical floor plan hosting the studio, 2-bedroom and 3-bedroom units. This floor plan is repeated and stacked from levels 2-7.
Image 4 Alt/Description: This section reveals the relationship between The Interstice and the adjacent building in relation to the human figure. It also shows the landscaping of the space inbetween the buildings.
Image 5 Alt/Description: The Unit Plans composed of a mix of studio, 2-bedroom, and 3-bedroom units. Units were designed with privacy as a driving design factor, as bedrooms are located on the North Side, away from the exterior corridor on the south.
Image 6 Alt/Description: This detail demonstrates the hybrid mass timber approach that this project applies. It features thermally broken balcony corridors and the exterior wall assembly.
Image 9 Caption: View of Path between the Interstice and adjacent building

Image Description/Alt Text\n: Massing concept sketch
Home Studio\n: ReZoned: Adaptive Housing on Underused Church Land — British Columbia Institute of Technology
Tags: Housing design, Shared Spaces, Co-Housing, Row Housing, Infill Housing, Mixed Use
Project Description:
Designed in collaboration with Alessandro Di Bari, the site seeks to reinterpret the spatial qualities of the traditional European city square such as enclosure, walkability, and social interaction, then translating these principles into the contemporary urban fabric of Vancouver. The nearby Immigrant Services Society of BC building and the relocated church are incorporated into the perimeter of the square, functioning as its walls and creating an enclosed yet permeable space. 

The Mini-square, located on the east parcel of the site and closest to the church further enforces the concept of the European square through its symmetry, brickwork, and articulated vertical and horizontal framing. The southeastern building includes a community kitchen and rooftop balcony, providing shared spaces to support the congregation, residents, and the broader local community.

Home Studio\n: ReZoned: Adaptive Housing on Underused Church Land — British Columbia Institute of Technology
Tags: Housing design, Shared Spaces, Co-Living, Courtyard Housing
Project Description: The wall of Resilience is a residential project that is organized based on the shape of the property lines and it fosters an experience of transitioning from public community landscape and an indoor community dining space to semi private inner courtyard. The housing typology organizes the outdoor amenity space to be above ground on the second and fifth floors to provide a wider view spot for the outdoor space. The ground floor offers a two level elevated bike storage that has a capacity of 90 units of bike storage. This project tests how the landscape can become a combination of formal green fields for hosting wedding ceremonies for the church that is integrated with a public hardscape plaza for the visitors. The building is shaped to be a stepping form near the west entry to avoid shadows being cast onto the ISS immigrant society. Shading devices such as slidable wooden louvers are attached to the floor edge of the single loaded corridor to reduce the solar gain from the south during the summer season. The single loaded corridor has guardrails that allows residents to grow their own plantings on which creates a green south and west facade.
Image 1 Alt/Description: A Rendered View showing the spatial organization of the site and the surrounding building heights.
Image 2 Alt/Description: The massing of the building started with angling a portion of the wall to create the central courtyard. The height of the building is changed to allow for southern solar gain. The west side is then being cut out to create the outdoor space on the upper levels to avoid shadows being cast onto the ISS Immigrant society.
Image 3 Alt/Description: The site is organized from a public space transitioned to a private courtyard by a wide pathway that defines the main walking area.
Image 4 Alt/Description: These are the typical floor plan layouts that include a unit mix of one bed, two bed, and three bedroom units. The ground floor has  a community dining spot located on the west entryway. The majority two bedroom and three bedroom units are located in the upper level above the fifth floor to ensure sufficient daylight getting in for the bigger units.
Image 5 Alt/Description: The unit layout is organized by the transition from public to private in which residents access the unit from living room to dining area and bedroom.
Image 6 Alt/Description: The majority of the 3 bedroom units are for supporting long term accommodation for the refugee families and it is located in the upper levels.
Image 7 Alt/Description: The North and South Section illustrates the human movements connected with the surrounding landscape where multiple pathways meets from different direction that connects the communal kitchen and dining to the church and the hardscape plaza.

Project Title: Thresholds of Resilience: Sheltering the Vulnerable, Without Isolation
Image Description/Alt Text\n: A rendering of the courtyard
Home Studio\n: ReZoned: Adaptive Housing on Underused Church Land — British Columbia Institute of Technology
Tags: Cooperative housing, Housing design, Option 9, Housing Production, Shared Spaces, Programmed Circulation, Flexible Spaces, Co-Housing, Co-Living, Courtyard Housing, Infill Housing
Project Description:
How do we house people who have lost their homes without cutting them off from the life of a community? This question sits at the heart of Thresholds of Resilience. Responding to the "ReZoned" challenge, the project transforms underused church land into a place where below-market housing and refugee housing are not "added on" but woven in.

The masterplan starts by strengthening the existing neighborhood. A carefully renovated church is joined by a new residential block. By removing private parking, the site becomes a pedestrian-first "Shared Ground" - not a plaza in name only, but a daily gathering space with a community kitchen where church members, ISSofBC staff, and all residents can cook, eat, and linger together. This is not an event space; it is an everyday place. At street level, this thick wall opens into an inhabited colonnade - a covered walkway where neighbors can pause, meet, or simply sit without feeling exposed to the street's noise. 

The courtyard designed for calm, not spectacle. Gentle water features mask city sounds. A canopy of pink cherry blossoms - chosen to honor the magnolia's quiet symbolism of resilience and home-offers shade and beauty. The courtyard is shared by all residents, safety here comes from mutual presence, not surveillance.

This thinking continues inside. Single-loaded corridors are facing the courtyard, act like elevated green walkway. These are places to nod hello, to see children playing below, to feel part of something. A simple structural grid allows for diverse 1-to-4-bedroom homes, with refugee housing distributed across all floors, indistinguishable from market units. Equity is not announced; it is built. Instead of exposed balconies, winter gardens are carved into the façade, private, sheltered, but still connected to the life of the building.

In the end, this project is about housing as a foundation for everyday community where people can finally rest and once again, be resilient and hopeful.
Image 1 Alt/Description: Image 1 depicts the diagrams of the site concept on how the massing evolved
Image 2 Alt/Description: Circulation Plan showing how pedestrian, cyclist, and garbage truck circulate on site.
Image 2 Caption: CIRCULATION PLAN: This drawing highlights the site's porous nature, delineating distinct pathways for pedestrians, cyclists, and essential services. The design prioritizes safe, intuitive movement between the residential block, the renovated church, and the adjacent ISSofBC.
Image 3 Alt/Description: Three renderings including 1. Formal garden, 2. Plaza, 3. Courtyard
Image 3 Caption: ATMOSPHERIC PERSPECTIVES: (1) View from the Formal Garden: A perspective showing the interface between the structured landscape of the Church and the residential volume, emphasizing the "bone-white" GFRC facade and its relationship to the heritage context. (2) View toward the Flex Plaza: This view captures the "inhabited colonnade," showcasing how the architectural threshold provides a porous yet protected edge for neighborhood interaction and play. (3) View from the Internal Courtyard: A visualization of the tranquil interior heart of the project, where water features and cherry blossoms create a restorative environment shared by all residents.
Image 4 Alt/Description: Longitudinal site section showing height variations of adjacent projects.
Image 4 Caption: SECTION A-A: A longitudinal section revealing the building's relationship to the property lines and the scale comparison between the existing Church, the ISSofBC, and the new residential building.
Image 5 Alt/Description: Exterior Rendering - SouthWest View
Image 5 Caption: STREET VIEW (SOUTHWEST PERSPECTIVE): A pedestrian-eye rendering that illustrates the building's soft embrace of the site and the "inhabited colonnade" that mitigates urban noise while inviting community pause.
Image 6 Alt/Description: Diagram explain the flexibility of the layout, in kits of part
Image 6 Caption: MODULAR EQUITY: The project utilizes a systematic structural grid to allow for "infinite permutation" of unit sizes, from single to quadruple bedroom homes.
Image 7 Alt/Description: main floor plan and typical second floor plan
Image 7 Caption: FLOOR PLAN ORGANIZATION: The layout emphasizes the open-air, green walkways that replace traditional dark hallways to foster a sense of mutual presence.
Image 8 Caption: UNIT TYPOLOGIES: These plans showcase how refugee housing (distributed throughout) is indistinguishable from market units. Key features include "winter gardens" carved into the facade, offering private, sheltered outdoor space that maintains a connection to the building’s collective life.
Image 9 Caption: SECTION B-B: A detailed cross-section highlighting the vertical life of the building, from the active ground floor to the residential units above, illustrating the single-loaded corridors that overlook the shared courtyard.
Image 10 Caption: PHYSICAL MASSING MODEL: Monochromatic exploration of the project's primary volumes and urban presence.

Image Description/Alt Text\n: Interior courtyard view, entrance to community space
Home Studio\n: ReZoned: Adaptive Housing on Underused Church Land — British Columbia Institute of Technology
Tags: Housing design
Project Description:
This project is part of the larger Cascade Residence development, organized as a linear housing bar that frames a continuous shared outdoor space. The design transforms a typical residential strip into a social spine, where circulation, landscape, and community life intersect.

Ground-level pathways and green spaces encourage interaction and movement, while the articulated massing breaks down the scale and creates rhythm along the length of the building. Units are arranged to maximize light, views, and connection to the shared space.

The project explores how linear living can support a more connected and community-driven residential experience.
Image 1 Alt/Description: The north façade of Courtyard Spine presents a rhythmic composition of mid-rise residential blocks organized along a continuous elevation. Variations in height, material tone, and window placement articulate each segment while maintaining an overall cohesive identity. Alternating light and dark cladding emphasizes the modular nature of the design, while recessed and projecting volumes create depth and shadow across the façade. A central break in the massing suggests a key പ്രവേശ or shared circulation point, reinforcing the idea of a connected spine that links the development together.
Image 1 Caption: Courtyard Spine – North Façade: A layered composition of modular residential blocks unified through rhythm and material contrast.
Image 2 Alt/Description: This site plan illustrates the internal courtyard of Courtyard Spine, organized as a linear landscaped sequence running through the center of the development. The space is defined by the surrounding building mass, which steps in and out to create a series of pockets and expansions along the courtyard edge. A meandering pedestrian pathway connects key nodes, including seating areas, planted zones, and small gathering spaces. A central feature—likely a plaza or focal installation—anchors the composition, while softer landscaping elements such as trees and shrubs create a more intimate, human-scaled environment. The layout balances movement and pause, encouraging both circulation and social interaction within a semi-enclosed green spine.
Image 3 Alt/Description: The south façade of Courtyard Spine presents a unified, light-clad surface articulated through stepped massing and a scattered arrangement of window openings. This seemingly irregular pattern is intentional—it translates the interior courtyard language onto the exterior. The distribution of windows reflects the beginning of the meandering path found within the landscaped courtyard, acting as an architectural expression of movement across the building’s surface. A recessed central entrance anchors the elevation, while the façade as a whole becomes a subtle diagram of the spatial experience unfolding behind it.
Image 4 Alt/Description: An east–west section of Courtyard Spine showing stepped residential units organized around a central courtyard, with integrated terraces and vertical circulation cores.
Image 5 Alt/Description: Floor plan of level 1 showing community space in the center highlighted in orange and 2-4 bed units surrounding it bike room, garbage room and mechanical room near the east entrance

Image Description/Alt Text\n: This is the view of my building as it appears from the main busy intersection.
Home Studio\n: Graduating Project — British Columbia Institute of Technology
Tags: Housing design, Shared Spaces, Flexible Spaces, Co-Housing, Co-Living, Courtyard Housing, Mixed Use
Project Description: This project explores the design of a contemporary building positioned at a prominent, high-traffic intersection, responding to both urban intensity and human-scale experience. The proposal aims to create a visually engaging yet context-sensitive structure that enhances the streetscape while maintaining a sense of openness and permeability. Through careful massing, material selection, and façade articulation, the building reduces its perceived bulk and integrates harmoniously with its surroundings. The design prioritizes accessibility, functionality, and sustainability, offering a dynamic space that serves both its users and the broader community while contributing positively to the urban fabric.
Image 1 Alt/Description: Columbia Elevation
Image 1 Caption: A mid-rise mixed-use building that mediates between historic low-rise context and contemporary urban density, featuring a layered façade, recessed balconies, and a transparent ground plane that activates the street along a key city intersection.
Image 2 Alt/Description: McKenzie Elevation
Image 2 Caption: A stepped residential form responding to the site’s slope, integrating layered terraces and articulated façade modules to reduce visual mass while creating varied outdoor spaces. The elevated podium activates the street edge with a public interface, reinforcing connectivity between the building and its urban landscape.
Image 3 Alt/Description: Front St Mews Elevation
Image 3 Caption: A layered mixed-use elevation that negotiates between the existing urban fabric and a larger residential mass, featuring a strong podium base and a rhythm of articulated modules above. Setbacks and terraces introduce permeability and light, while the activated ground level reinforces pedestrian engagement along the street edge.
Image 4 Alt/Description: Courtyard View
Image 4 Caption: A biophilic courtyard connects residents through layered greenery, warm timber pathways, and open circulation, creating a calm social retreat within the building.
Image 5 Alt/Description: Library View
Image 5 Caption: A bright interior library space combines natural light, simple finishes, and flexible study zones to support quiet learning and community interaction.
